coredata.dc:title: AgNP anchored carbon dots and chitin nanowhisker embedded soy protein isolate films with freshness preservation for active packaging
coredata.dc:creator: Koshy, Rekha Rose
Reghunadhan, Arunima
Mary, Siji K.
Sadanandan, Sandeep
Jose, Seno
Thomas, Sabu
Pothen, Laly A.
coredata.dc:description: Biodegradable substitutes to the commonly practiced packaging materials are always in high demand. Active biopolymer film was obtained from soy-protein isolate (SPI) and the antibacterial properties were introduced by incorporating silver nanoparticles. Active bio-composite films incorporating silver nanoparticles (AgNP) are much sought after in the food packaging industry owing to their ability to extend the shelf life of fresh foods. But the toxicity of the ingredients used for preparation (mainly the reducing agents) and the poor stability limits their usage for the aforementioned application. Herein, to counter these limitations, we have adopted an environment-friendly method where carbon dot (CD) prepared from soy protein isolate was used to prepare AgNPs. AgNPs (1% wt.) and different concentrations of chitin nano whisker (CNW) (2–10% wt.) were added to soy protein isolate to fabricate nanocomposite films. The subsequent effect on the latter’s tensile property, moisture content, thermal property and morphological characteristics were noted. Here, for the first time, the steam explosion technique was used for the preparation of CNW which resulted in highly crystalline CNW with good antibacterial properties. It was observed that the mechanical strength and thermal stability of SPI film were significantly increased and the moisture content decreased with the addition of CNW and AgNP. The synergistic effect of AgNP and CNW imparted excellent antioxidant, antibacterial and antifungal properties to the SPI film thus enabling the SPI film to extend the shelf life of the food material that was packed using the same. Commercial production of such SPI films would revolutionize the food packing industry given its health benefits and its cost-effectiveness.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Soy protein isolate
Chitin nano whisker
Silver nanoparticle
Antioxidant
Antibacterial
Antifungal

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of AgNP- anchored carbon dot
Characterization of nanoparticles and composite films
Possibility to be used as packaging
Structural characterization of the nanocomposite films
Thickness, moisture content and water solubility
Specific migration of the silver nanoparticles
Monitoring antifungal activity for packaging application

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: . TEM and HRTEM images of AgNP. The inset image in (B) shows the size distribution histogram with a Gaussian fitting curve.
SEM micrographs of (A) SPI, (B) SPI-8CNW, (C) SPI-AgNP (D) SPI-CNW-AgNP films.
(A) FTIR and (B) XRD of SPI and SPI composites.
(a)TGA curve, (b) The DPPH radical scavenging activity and (c) antibacterial activity of SPI, SPI-AgNP, SPI-CNW and SPI-CNW-AgNP nanocomposite films.

Antifungal activity of SPI and SPI composites against Rhizopus. A) Plate containing control fungi only, B) fungi + SPI, C) fungi + SPI-CNW film, D) fungi+ SPI-AgNP film and E) fungi+ SPI-CNW-AgNP film.
Results obtained for biodegradation analysis of the films.
Photographs of packed bread in SPI, PE and SPI-CNW-AgNP pouches.
The possible interaction between the synthesized carbon dots and silver nanoparticles.
Fabrication of CD-AgNP/CNW- SPI polymer films for packaging.
Thickness, tensile strength, elongation at break and moisture content value of SPI and SPI composite films.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Values are stated as mean ± SD. The different superscript a-f show a significant difference between values (p ˂0.05).

head.ce:title: AgNP anchored carbon dots and chitin nanowhisker embedded soy protein isolate films with freshness preservation for active packaging

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Postgraduate and Research Department of Chemistry, CMS College (Affiliated to Mahatma Gandhi University)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biodegradable substitutes to the commonly practiced packaging materials are always in high demand. Active biopolymer film was obtained from soy-protein isolate (SPI) and the antibacterial properties were introduced by incorporating silver nanoparticles. Active bio-composite films incorporating silver nanoparticles (AgNP) are much sought after in the food packaging industry owing to their ability to extend the shelf life of fresh foods. But the toxicity of the ingredients used for preparation (mainly the reducing agents) and the poor stability limits their usage for the aforementioned application. Herein, to counter these limitations, we have adopted an environment-friendly method where carbon dot (CD) prepared from soy protein isolate was used to prepare AgNPs. AgNPs (1% wt.) and different concentrations of chitin nano whisker (CNW) (2–10% wt.) were added to soy protein isolate to fabricate nanocomposite films. The subsequent effect on the latter’s tensile property, moisture content, thermal property and morphological characteristics were noted. Here, for the first time, the steam explosion technique was used for the preparation of CNW which resulted in highly crystalline CNW with good antibacterial properties. It was observed that the mechanical strength and thermal stability of SPI film were significantly increased and the moisture content decreased with the addition of CNW and AgNP. The synergistic effect of AgNP and CNW imparted excellent antioxidant, antibacterial and antifungal properties to the SPI film thus enabling the SPI film to extend the shelf life of the food material that was packed using the same. Commercial production of such SPI films would revolutionize the food packing industry given its health benefits and its cost-effectiveness.

The films fabricated from SPI embedded with carbon dot anchored AgNPs and Chitin nanowhiskers retard the activity of the fungi and preserve the food freshness
ga1

ce:section.ce:para: Hectic modern-day lifestyle, coupled with a growing need for consumption of fresh-cut vegetables, fruits and cooked food on the go, has generated the pressing need for safe packaging material. However, the safety and quality of such packaging material is always a matter of suspicion with the food materials thus packed being generally susceptible to bacterial and microbial contamination (Saravanakumar et al., 2020). Contamination by pathogens such as Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus aureus, Listeria monocytogenes and Aspergillus Niger can cause gastric health problems and even death (Sarengaowa et al., 2020). Hence new techniques are being used to increase the shelf-life of the food items, the most important among them being the use of antibacterial biopolymer coating and packaging films.

Compared to synthetic polymers, the use of biopolymer-based packaging has grown and several biopolymeric matrices are available for biodegradable packaging. Soy protein, a byproduct of soybean oil production, is one such matrix. Soy protein, based on its protein content, is classified as soy flour, soy protein concentrates and soy protein isolate. Soy protein isolate [SPI] containing 90% protein is the most commonly used form for film formation (Yu et al., 2019). However, due to its inherent hydrophilicity, soy protein by itself cannot produce good polymeric films (Kumar et al., 2010). Hence, nanofillers, both organic and inorganic are added to the SPI matrix. Chitin nano whisker (CNW) isolated from chitin is a filler commonly used in biopolymeric matrices due to its natural abundance, low cost, high aspect ratio, biodegradability, non-toxicity, crystallinity etc. (Salaberria et al., 2015) Chitin is the second most abundant polymer found in the exoskeleton of crabs, shrimps, prawns and insects, and the cell walls of yeast, fungi and mushrooms. CNW is extracted from chitin using methods such as acid hydrolysis and TEMPO-oxidation (Anwer et al., 2019). However, all the reported methods are time-consuming and require several days (Salaberria et al., 2015). Though the reinforcing effect of CNW on biopolymer films such as starch (Qin et al., 2016; Salaberria et al., 2014), carboxymethyl cellulose (Oun & Rhim, 2019), gelatin (Sahraee et al., 2017), etc. has been widely reported, not much work has been done to study the effect of the addition of CNW on SPI matrix (Lu et al., 2004).

Silver nanoparticle (AgNP) is an inorganic antibacterial agent commonly added to biopolymeric matrices to enhance the antibacterial property and thermal stability (Bang et al., 2019). Moreover, the antibacterial property of AgNPs is found to be significantly affected by its shape, size and also the method through which it is synthesized (Jin et al., 2015). Hence, a variety of synthetic routes have been envisaged to synthesize AgNPs with better antibacterial properties (López-Carballo et al., 2013). However, commonly reported synthetic procedures are either high energy-consuming or employ strong reducing agents leading to an uncontrollable reduction process. Meanwhile, in the absence of good stabilizing agents, the formed nanoparticles tend to easily aggregate leading to a reduction in antibacterial properties. To synthesize AgNPs using the reducing property of carbon dot (CD) particles is a good method to obtain stable AgNPs without an extra stabilizing agent (Jin et al., 2015; Jana et al., 2015). The AgNP gets anchored on the CD surface and the numerous OH and COOH groups on the surface of CD bind with Ag+ and stabilize AgNPs (Jin et al., 2015).

The objective of this study is to prepare, characterize and examine the reinforcing effect of SPI- based bio nanocomposites on the addition of CNW / AgNP / CNW-AgNP mixture. This is the first time such an attempt on the reinforcing effect of AgNPs- anchored carbon dots along with CNW in a soy protein matrix was made. The effects of the addition of AgNPs, different concentrations of CNW and a CNW/AgNP combo on the morphological, chemical and physical properties of SPI films were investigated. The effects of the addition of CNW/AgNP on the antioxidant, antibacterial and antifungal characteristics of SPI films were also investigated.

SPI (protein content > 90%) was purchased from Adeesh Agro Foods, Haryana. AgNO3 was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Dried chitin shrimp shell flakes (Poly-(1−4)-β-N-acetyl-D-glucosamine) were purchased from Marine Chemicals, Kochi. All the chemicals used were of analytical grade. For antibacterial analysis, four microbial species were used: Gram-positive bacteria, Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC 25923); and Enterococcus faecalis (ATCC 29212); Bacillus, Gram-negative bacteria, Escherichia coli (ATCC 25922); and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (ATCC 27853). For antifungal analysis, fungal strain Rhizopus was used. They were obtained from the American Type Culture Collection and were cultured on NB agar (Himedia, India).

Carbon dot from SPI was prepared using the method adopted by Fang et al. (2016). In this process, 2.5 g of SPI in 20 mL water was heated in a hydrothermal autoclave at 180 ºC for 6 h. After cooling the autoclave, the resulting brown solution was centrifuged at a high speed (10,000 rpm) for 15 min, followed by the filtration of the upper brown solution using a 0.22 µm filter membrane. The solution was then dried and diluted with 50 mL water and stored at 4 ºC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of AgNP- anchored carbon dot
ce:section.ce:para: AgNP anchored carbon dots were prepared following the method adopted by Jana et al. (2015). 2 mL of AgNO3 solution was taken from 15 mM stock solution and added to 6 mL of CD solution and mixed thoroughly. The mixture was then placed in a UV chamber (365 nm) for 5 min. The solution immediately turned to light brown, indicating the hybridization of AgNPs. This hybridized particle was labeled as AgNP. The resulting solution was centrifuged, freeze-dried to obtain a solid mass, which could be easily dispersed in water when needed. The anchoring is schematically given in Scheme 1.

The procedures reported (Lu et al., 2013; Fan et al., 2008) to date for the isolation of CNW from raw chitin are tedious, take several days for completion and use an acidic medium to prevent agglomeration. Hence, we have adopted a new technique [IN patent 329388] for the isolation of CNW; using this, highly crystalline CNW can be extracted from raw chitin within a day under neutral conditions. This method involves the application of steam explosion technique using an autoclave for the extraction of CNW and involves three steps; deproteinization, demineralization and bleaching. In the deproteinization step, 40 gm of raw chitin flakes were treated with 3 N NaOH under pressure in an autoclave for 30 min to remove the protein. After washing the resulting suspension, demineralization was carried out wherein it was treated with 2 N HCl in the same autoclave for half an hour. After releasing the pressure immediately and washing the suspension to neutral pH, bleaching was done using sodium chlorite water mixture (1:3). After bleaching, the suspension was thoroughly washed with distilled water and sonicated (Labman probe sonicator, PRO 250), to facilitate whisker formation.

SPI and SPI based composite films were prepared using the solution casting technique (Kanmani and Rhim, 2014). To prepare SPI film solution, 6 g of SPI powder was dissolved in 100 mL distilled water and 45% (w/w of SPI) of glycerol was added as the plasticizer. The solution was heated at 80 ºC for 30 min. The film-forming solution was poured onto a Teflon-coated pan and dried in a vacuum oven overnight. For the preparation of SPI-CNW composite films, different concentrations of CNW (2%, 4%, 6%, 8%, 10% (w/w) of dry weight of SPI) were added to the film-forming SPI solution and stirred at a temperature of 60 ºC for half an hour. For the preparation of SPI-CNW-AgNP composite film, 1% AgNPs (w/w of SPI) was added to the film-forming solution containing SPI and 8% CNW. The 8% CNW were selected as it gave the highest tensile strength to SPI film and 1% AgNPs were selected based on previous reports (Jafari et al., 2016). The film-forming solutions were poured onto a Teflon plate (diameter 16 cm) and dried in a vacuum oven overnight. All the dried films were preconditioned at 50 ± 5% RH for 48 h before analysis.
A schematic representation of the film formation is given as scheme 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of nanoparticles and composite films
ce:section.ce:para: Morphological analysis of the nanoparticles was done using a Transmission Electron microscope (TEM). The absorbance of CDs and AgNPs was measured using UV-Visible double beam spectrometer model V-530 (Jasco, Japan) in the scan range 190–900 nm.

Fluorescence spectra of CDs and AgNPs were analyzed using a Shimadzu PL spectrophotometer operated at 280–450 nm range of excitation wavelengths. The surface morphology of SPI bio composite films was observed using SEM (Carl Zeiss Secondary Electron Microscope) operating at an accelerating voltage of 20 kV.

XRD of CNW and SPI composite films was done using Siemens X-ray diffractometer D5000 instrument using Ni-filtered Cu Kα radiation operating at 40 kV and 30 mA. The degree of crystallinity of CNW was calculated using Segal Equation (Segal et al., 1959). The crystal size of CNW was calculated using the Scherrer equation (Shankar and Rhim, 2016).

Functional groups in CNW and SPI composites were observed using FTIR spectroscopy (Perkin Elmer FTIR spectrometer with Universal ATR accessory) in the scan range of 4000–500 cm−1 and resolution 4 cm−1.

The thermal stability of the samples was analyzed using a thermogravimetric analyzer (Shimadzu TGA-Q500) working at a heating rate of 10 ºC/min in the range 20–900 ºC. The thickness of films was determined using a micrometer (Dial thickness gauge 7301, Mitutoyo, Japan) at an accuracy of 0.01 mm. Three random measurements were taken for each film and the average values were taken.

The moisture content of composite films was measured using a digital moisture analyser of MA50. X2. A series. Triplicate measurement was done. Water solubility was determined following the method of Jamróz et al. (2019) with slight modifications. Film samples (5 × 5 cm) were dried in an air oven for 24 h until a constant weight was attained (W1). Afterwards, the film samples were immersed in 50 mL distilled water for 24 h. After this period the remaining film samples were picked out, and dried to constant weight in an air oven at 70 ºC for 24 h and weighed (W2). Film solubility was calculated as
(1)
S o lub i l i t y ( % ) =
w 1 − w 2
w 1
× 100

Tensile properties of each film (3 each) were measured according to the standard method of ASTM D 882–88 using Instron Universal Testing Machine (Model 5565, Instron Engineering Corporation, Canton, MA, USA). The ability to release antioxidant components from SPI.

composite films were studied using the DPPH [2,2-diphenyl-1-picryl-hydrazyl-hydrate] assay. For this, around 10 mg of each film were immersed in 4 mL of 100 µM DPPH methanol solution. After reacting in the dark, the absorbance of the reaction solution was recorded at 517 nm. DPPH scavenging activity was calculated as
(2)
R a d i c a l
s c a v e n g i n g
a c t i v i t y ( % ) =
A 0 − A
A 0
× 100
where A0 = absorbance of the control and A = absorbance of the sample.

The antimicrobial activity of both CNW solution and SPI composite films were evaluated using the disk diffusion method. For this, bacterial strains, Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), Escherichia coli (E. coli), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa), Bacillus were used. Antibiotics amikacin (AK) discs (30 μg antibiotic per tablet) was used as the positive control. After overnight incubation in the dark at 32.5 ºC for the bacterial strains, zones of inhibition (ZOI) around each disc were measured with a digital calliper. The antifungal activity was measured using the dual culture technique. In this method, an agar disc containing Rhizopus isolate was placed on one half of the PDA culture plate and the sample film was placed on the other half of the same plate. As a control, Rhizopus was placed similarly on a fresh PDA plate. All pairings were carried out in quadruplicate and incubated at 28 °C. Antifungal activity was tested 4 days after incubation by measuring the radius of the Rhizopus colony in the direction of the sample film (R2) and the radius of the Rhizopus colony in the control plate (R1). The two readings were transformed into percentage inhibition of radial growth (PIRG) using the formula,
(3)
P I R G ( P e r c e n t a g e
I n h i b i t i o n
o f
R a d i a l
G r o w t h ) =
R 1 − R 2
R 1
The number of days required to overgrow the whole colony of Rhizopus was recorded.
Water vapor permeability.

Water vapor permeability of the films were determined gravimetrically according to ASTM E96–95. According to this method, film sample (7 cm × 7 cm) was mounted on a cup (6.8 cm diameter and 2.5 cm depth) containing 18 mL distilled water and sealed. The cups were placed in a humidity chamber maintained at 25 °C and 50% RH and the weight changes of the cups were measured at 1 h interval for 8 h. The WVP (gm./m.2. Pa. s) of the film was calculated as
(4)
W V P =
w × x
t x A x Δ p
where W = increased weight of test vessel (g), x = thickness (m), t = time (s) for weight change of the cup, A= film permeation area (m2), and ΔP is the partial water vapor pressure difference across two sides of the film.

Soil biodegradation analysis was done according to the method suggested by Arancibia et al. (2014) with some modifications. For this, the samples were cut into (2 cm × 2 cm), dried in an air oven at 105ºC for 24 h and weighed (W1). The films were then buried in plastic boxes at a depth of 10 cm from the soil to ensure aerobic degradation. The analysis was done at 32 ºC at a relative humidity of 35% by adding water periodically. The excess water was drained off through a hole at the bottom of the box. After the stipulated period, the samples were taken out from the soil, washed gently to wipe out the soil particles and dried in the oven at 105ºC for a period of 12 h and weighed (W2). The extent of degradation was measured in terms of % weight loss using the following
(5)
W L ( % ) =
w 1 − w 2
w 1
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possibility to be used as packaging
ce:section.ce:para: The packaging applicability of SPI and SPI-CNW-AgNP films was studied for bread slices and their ability to prevent fungal contamination on the bread was monitored for a period of 10 days. Rectangular pouches of film samples (10 cm length and 8 cm width) were made and bread pieces of size 2 cm × 2 cm were packed inside. A pouch made from low-density polyethylene (LDPE) containing bread slices served as control. The pouches were kept at room temperature and monitored daily for any visible fungal growth for 15 days. The experiment was repeated thrice.
One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was performed with Duncan’s multiple range tests using the SPSS statistical analysis reference.

In this work, SPI has been used as a precursor to produce the CD. The surface of CD produced from SPI will be covered with functional groups such as -OH, -NH2 etc., which can donate electrons to Ag+ and reduce it to Ag (0). In this process, CD acts as a template to which Ag(0) can anchor (Chen et al., 2016). On mixing AgNO3 with CD, a yellow solution was formed which when placed under UV turned brown within minutes.

The color change indicated the complete nucleation and growth of AgNPs on CDs. HRTEM image ( Fig. 1) shows that each synthesized AgNP is anchored on each CD. Size histogram shows that most of the AgNP are in the range of 12–14 nm in size. The absorbance peak of CD and the synthesized AgNP are shown in (Fig. S1(A) in supporting information). CD shows a strong absorption at 206 nm due to the π-π * transition of aromatic C = C bonds. A small hump at 260 nm is attributed to the n-π * transition of CO bonds (Chen et al., 2016). The UV-Visible spectra of AgNP show an absorption band at 482 nm attributed to surface plasmon absorption of AgNP (Shen et al., 2013). However, the fluorescence of CD was completely quenched by adding AgNO3 solution (supplementary (Fig. S1(B) in supporting information). Since AgNP is formed by utilizing the reducing property of AgNPs, electrons transfer from CD to Ag (I) takes place, resulting in the quenching of fluorescence (Jana et al., 2015). It is observed that no color change or agglomeration happened for the colloidal solution of AgNPs even after storage for 50 days. This indicate that CDs can serve as a stabilizing agent for AgNPs and prevent its aggregation.

TEM images of CNW are shown in Fig. 2(A). These reveal that CNW suspension consists of aggregated and individual nanocrystals. The individual crystals had a needle-like morphology with a wide range of length distribution ranging from 100 to 400 nm. The XRD pattern of CNW is shown in Fig. 2(B). The diffractogram shows typical peaks at 2θ values 19º, 9º, 23º corresponding to highly crystalline CNW. The absence of diffraction peak at 29.6º shows the complete removal of calcium carbonate during the demineralization step. The presence of well-defined diffraction peaks proves the existence of crystalline CNW with a crystallinity index value of 99.67%. The crystal size calculated from the Scherrer equation was between 5 nm and 9 nm. The UV absorption spectra of CNW (Fig. 2(C)) shows a maximum absorption band at 194 nm owing to the presence of chromophore groups glucosamine and N-acetyl glucosamine. The absence of absorption band at 280 nm due to aromatic amino acid groups shows that complete protein removal occurred during the deproteinization step. The FTIR spectrum of CNW is given in Fig. 2(D). The characteristic absorption band of CNW such as OH stretching band at 3372 cm−1, NH stretching band at 3259 cm−1, CH3 and CH2 stretching band at 2877 cm−1, amide I band at 1649 and 1626 cm−1, amide II at 1556 cm−1, methyl CH stretch at 1379 cm−1 and 1313 cm−1 and amide III bands at 1243 cm−1, were observed (Oun and Rhim, 2017). The peak at 1153 cm−1 corresponds to glycosidic C-O-C stretching. Strong peaks at 1022 and 1070 cm−1 are attributed to the saccharide structure present in the carbohydrate backbone. A peak at 935 cm−1corresponds to the β-linkage of the glucopyranose ring of chitin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of the nanocomposite films

ce:section.ce:para: The energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) spectra of SPI-AgNP (Fig. S2 in supporting information) shows a weak signal at 3KeV was detected indicating the entrapment of AgNP in the SPI matrix. The signal intensity was weak due to the low concentration of AgNP in the matrix. Fig. 2 shows the SEM micrographs of SPI, SPI-CNW, SPI-AgNP and SPI-CNW-AgNP composite films. Pure SPI film exhibited a homogenous and smooth surface without any pores or cracks, providing evidence of the high film-forming property of SPI. The incorporation of CNW (Fig. 2(B)) in the SPI matrix made the surface compact and rough, indicating the interaction between SPI and CNW. Wu et al. (Wu et al., 2020) observed similar morphological changes when diatomite was incorporated into the SPI matrix. In the SPI films with AgNPs (Fig. 2(C)), flower-like AgNPs was found to be uniformly distributed in the SPI matrix. Compared to SPI-CNW and SPI-AgNP film, SPI-CNW-AgNP film exhibited a unique surface morphology without any visible aggregation of CNW or AgNPs (Fig. 2(D)).

FTIR spectrum of SPI, SPI-CNW, SPI-AgNP and SPI-CNW-AgNP composites are shown in Fig. 3 (A). The control SPI contains characteristic bands at 3100–3500 cm−1 attributed to free and bound OH and NH stretching vibrations, 2929 cm−1 assigned to CH asymmetric stretching vibrations, 1631 cm−1 assigned to amide I band due to CO stretching vibrations,1537 cm−1 attributed to amide II band corresponding to NH bending and CH deformation, 1398 cm−1 corresponding to CH3 deformation and 1235 cm−1 attributed to amide III band corresponding to NH bending and CN stretching. Peaks below 1200 cm−1 correspond to the fingerprint region. As both SPI and CNW contain the same amide and amino groups, the FTIR spectrum of the two shows similarity in the spectrum. Only slight changes in peak positions and intensity could be observed. The intensity of peak around 3268 cm−1 increased with increasing concentration of CNW showing hydrogen bond formation between SPI and CNW (Sahraee et al., 2017). A slight shift in the amide I and amide II band shows that changes have occurred in the secondary structure of protein on the addition of fillers (Goormaghtigh et al., 1994) which reveals that interactions have occurred between SPI and the fillers. Due to the very low concentration of AgNPs, not much change in band intensity or position could be observed on the addition of AgNPs. However, it has been reported that the acetamide and hydroxyl groups present in the structure of CNW will favor the attachment of AgNP onto its surface thus stabilizing Ag(0) nanoparticles (Oun and Rhim, 2017).

XRD patterns of SPI, SPI-CNW, SPI-AgNP and SPI-CNW-AgNP composites are shown in Fig. 3(B). The control SPI film has weak characteristic peaks at 10.2º and 22º, which show the low degree of crystallinity of the SPI matrix. At a lower concentration of CNW (< 4%) similar peaks were obtained for SPI and SPI-CNW, suggesting that the ordered structure of SPI was kept even after CNW addition, glycerol-plasticizing and solvent casting. This indicates that when a low concentration of CNW was added into the SPI matrix, it was dispersed homogeneously into the amorphous domains of the SPI matrix, maintaining the ordered structure of the SPI matrix. However, while the CNW content increased to 10 wt%, the peak at 22º and 10.2º shifted to 19º and 9º typically assigned to CNW. Increasing CNW content induces the self-aggregation of CNW, resulting in the size expansion of aggregated CNW domains. This results in the partial destruction of the ordered structure of SPI (Sahraee et al., 2017). XRD of AgNPs is reported to possess characteristic peaks at 36.2º, 43º, 63.6º and 76º. However, all the said peaks disappeared in SPI-AgNP nanocomposites. The peak intensity and position of pure SPI did not change in the presence of AgNPs. This prove that AgNPs is uniformly distributed in the SPI matrix and has good interaction with the matrix. In SPI-CNW-AgNP composites, the crystallinity of CNW was retained proving that CNW is more crystalline than AgNPs thereby increasing the overall crystallinity of the composites. A similar result was reported by Jafari et al. (2016) when AgNPs were incorporated into chitosan chitin composites.

The tensile strength and elongation at break values of SPI, SPI-CNW, SPI-AgNP and SPI-CNW-AgNP composites films are shown in Table 1. The tensile strength increased from 4.67 to 15.45 MPa with increasing CNW concentration from 0% to 8% and thereafter decreased for 10% CNW. The increase in tensile strength indicates that incorporation of CNW in SPI matrix results in strong interaction between filler and matrix, thereby restricting the movement of the matrix. The increase in tensile strength of SPI on the incorporation of the filler can be attributed to several reasons, such as the formation of a strong interpenetrating network by hydrogen bonds, higher stiffness and density of CNW in comparison to SPI matrix (Sahraee et al., 2017), increase in crystallinity of the matrix and filling of void spaces in amorphous regions. Compared to 8% CNW, the addition of 6% CNW resulted in super elongation of SPI film which can be attributed to the homogenous distribution of CNW in SPI resulting in enhanced extensibility (Zhai et al., 2017). Hence compared to 8% CNW, the addition of 6% CNW result in the homogenous film. Therefore, we have added AgNPs to SPI-8% CNW film to get the film with optimum tensile strength and elongation at break. The SPI composites with more than 8% CNW exhibited a decrease in tensile strength due to the agglomeration of nanoparticles at higher concentrations. Several other authors have also reported a decrease in tensile strength at a higher concentration of nanoparticles (Li et al., 2016; Sahraee et al., 2017). AgNPs were also found to enhance the tensile strength of the SPI matrix. The elongation percentage was found to decrease drastically for 8% CNW addition which shows that high intermolecular interactions between polar functional groups of SPI and CNW restrict the molecular motion of protein chains.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thickness, moisture content and water solubility
ce:section.ce:para: The thickness of the composite films (Table 1) increased with the increasing concentration of CNW and with the addition of AgNPs. This increase may be due to the increase in the solid content of the film. A similar result was observed for bio composite films made with sodium alginate, cellulose and copper oxide nanoparticles (Saravanakumar et al., 2020). The moisture content value of SPI and SPI composite films are shown in Table 1. The addition of nanofillers was found to significantly reduce the moisture content by about 50%. This may be due to the strong interactions between SPI and the nanofillers which in turn weakens the interaction between SPI and water molecules. The nanofillers also break the hydrogen bond formed between NH groups of SPI and water molecules. A similar decrease in moisture content was observed by Yu et al. (2018) when cellulose nanocrystal was added to the SPI matrix. The moisture content of SPI films varied significantly (p < 0.05) between different types of composite films and SPI-CNW-AgNP film was found to possess the lowest value. This can be attributed to the homogenous distribution of CNW and AgNPs in the matrix without any agglomeration. The solubility of the SPI films decreased from 55.12 ± 0.12–48.65 ± 0.25, 38.53 ± 0.82 and 28.46 ± 1.12 for SPI-AgNP, SPI-CNW, SPI-CNW-AgNP films respectively.

The thermal decomposition process of SPI, SPI-CNW, SPI-AgNP and SPI-CNW-AgNP composites are shown in the form of TG and DTG curves ( Fig. 4a). Pure SPI shows a three-step degradation process. The loss below 100 ºC is attributed to the loss of water molecules. Evaporation of glycerol occurs in the range 130–250 ºC, and decomposition of protein chains occurs in the range 250–500 ºC. The addition of CNW, AgNPs and CNW/AgNP was found to increase the maximum thermal degradation rate and residual weight and decrease the maximum degradation rate. This enhancement in thermal property on the addition of nanofillers shows that the nanofillers not only interacts with the protein chain leading to crosslinking but also improves the energy transfer, resulting in thermally stable composites (Qin et al., 2019). Among the SPI composites, SPI-CNW-AgNP composites displayed the lowest degradation rate and thus the highest thermal stability. In fact, at T70 (the temperature at 70% weight loss), the decomposition temperature of SPI-CNW-AgNP composite was 20–30 ºC higher than the control SPI. This clearly shows the enhanced thermal stability of SPI-CNW-AgNP composite films.

Fig. 4 (b) shows the DPPH radical scavenging ability of SPI and SPI composite films. As is evident from the figure, the DPPH radical scavenging ability of the SPI matrix increased significantly with the addition of CNW and/AgNPs, showing an enhancement in the antioxidant property of the films with the addition of nanofillers. In alcoholic stimulants, the DPPH radical scavenging activity of SPI films increased from 2.5% to 25.7% with the addition of both CNW and AgNPs. The antioxidant ability is attributed to the reduction of the radical DPPH in the presence of AgNPs and CNW. DPPH radicals can accept electrons or hydrogen from external molecules to form a stable diamagnetic molecule. AgNPs can donate the electron and the amide and hydroxyl group of CNW can react with the unstable radical to form a stable macromolecule (Anraku et al., 2015).

Antibacterial activity of packaging film is an important parameter that determines the efficiency of the material as an intelligent packaging material. The antibacterial result of SPI and SPI composite films against Bacillus and E. coli are shown in Fig. 4 (c). Disc diffusion method was tried out to determine the antibacterial activity of the prepared films. As evident from the figure, all the prepared films except the control SPI film exhibited good antibacterial activity against both gram-positive Bacillus and gram-negative E. coli bacteria. The SPI-CNW-AgNP film shows an inhibition zone diameter of 8 mm and 13 mm against Bacillus and E. coli respectively. Though many authors have reported (Bang et al., 2019; Raghavendra et al., 2016) high antibacterial activity of AgNPs against gram-negative bacteria such as E. coli, we could get good activity against both gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria. The combination of CNW and AgNPs is found to exhibit good antibacterial activity due to the synergistic effect of both components, which facilitate good activity despite low concentrations of each component in the matrix. The antibacterial activity of nanoparticles is expected to occur in three steps. First, the nanoparticles get attached to the outer cell membrane of bacteria disturbing normal functions such as fluidity and membrane permeability. Secondly, nanoparticles penetrate the cell membrane and interact with intracellular substances such as proteins, DNA and the respiratory chain. Finally, formation of reactive oxygen species and release of active ions take place, killing the bacteria.

The antifungal activity of SPI and SPI composite films are shown in Fig. 5 . PIRG (in percentage) was calculated after 5 days of incubation at 25 ºC. The PIRG value of SPI-CNW-AgNP was 62.5% which shows that SPI-CNW-AgNP films could be used as an antifungal packaging material. The antifungal activity of SPI composite film is due to the dissolution of nanoparticles in the fungal cell membrane leading to disruption of enzyme activity and ATP production affecting mycelial growth and germination of spores (Gonzoalez-Estrada et al., 2017).

The decrease in WVP is an important criterion which determines the applicability of biopolymer films for use in food coatings, packaging etc. The WVP of SPI and SPI nanocomposite films are shown in Table. The WVP value of control SPI film was (2.75 ± 0.16) x 10−9 g.m/m2. Pa. s, which decreased significantly (p < 0.05) on addition of CNW/AgNP. The lowest values (0.87 ± 0.33) and (0.54 ± 0.31) were obtained for SPI-6CNW and SPI-CNW-AgNP nanocomposite films. The decrease in WVP can be attributed to the homogenous distribution of nanoparticle in the polymer matrix which offers a torturous pathway for the water vapor while traveling through the film thereby increasing the path length for diffusion. The WVP values for SPI films with higher CNW concentrations (>6%) was slightly higher due to slight aggregation of CNW in SPI matrix which actually decreased the presence of CNW in some parts of the matrix thereby facilitating the permeability of water vapor through the matrix. However, on addition of both AgNPs and 8%CNW, the combined reinforcement of both the nanoparticles led to a further decrease in WVP. Qin et al.(Qin et al., 2016) also observed a similar trend in WVP which CNW was incorporated in maize starch film.
Table
Films WVP (10−9 g.m/m2. Pa. s)
SPI 2.75 ± 0.16a
SPI-2CNW 2.48 ± 0.49ab
SPI-4CNW 2.01 ± 0.11b
SPI-6CNW 0.87 ± 0.33de
SPI-8CNW 1.02 ± 0.16de
SPI-10CNW 1.44 ± 0.42 cd
SPI-AgNP 1.94 ± 0.28bc
SPI-CNW-AgNP 0.54 ± 0.31e

Biodegradability is an important parameter that should be measured in the case of packaging films. Fig. 7 shows the effect of AgNP/ CNW on the biodegradability rate of SPI nanocomposite films. It is observed that the biodegradability rate of pure SPI films increased up to 90% with the increase in burial time. However, the rate of biodegradability of nanocomposite film was slightly less than the control film. When CNW/AgNP was added to the SPI film, because of the strong interactions happening between SPI and the nanofillers, the degradation becomes difficult due to greater difficulty in breaking the bond between the matrix and the fillers. The lowest degradation rate was observed for SPI-CNW-AgNP film which indirectly hints the strong association of the two fillers with the matrix. Deepa et al.,(Deepa et al., 2016), also observed a similar decrease in the degradation of sodium alginate film on incorporation of cellulose nanofibril.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specific migration of the silver nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The specific migration of silver has been determined using an extract which has been prepared by digesting the samples using n-Heptane as a simulant at 38 ℃ for 30 min. The tests suggest that there was no specific migration of silver nanoparticles from the composite films. The details are presented in Table 2. It suggests that the films are much suitable and safe for the packaging applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring antifungal activity for packaging application
ce:section.ce:para: The practical applicability of SPI-CNW-AgNP film for packaging was studied using bread slices as shown in Fig. 7. Bread pieces when kept at room temperature get easily affected by fungus and Rhizopus stolonifera, commonly known as bread mould, is the main fungus attacking bread. It can be seen clearly from the figure that SPI and polyethene (PE) pouch could not protect the bread slice from fungal attack and the attack started from the 4th day of the packaging itself. No fungal growth was observed on bread slices packed in a pouch made with SPI-CNW-AgNP film and the bread remained fresh and retained its shape even on the 15th day of packaging which is comparatively longer than reported results on bread packaging by biopolymer matrices (Tan et al., 2015).

In summary, AgNPs and CNW were used to produce active SPI based nanocomposite films with improved properties. AgNPs were synthesized using the reducing and capping ability of the carbon dot solution. The incorporation of AgNPs and CNW increased the mechanical strength and thermal stability and decreased the moisture content of the film. The composite film exhibited significant antibacterial activity against both gram-positive bacillus and gram-negative E. coli bacteria. The film, thus prepared, when used as a packaging material was found to inhibit fungal growth on bread samples, thereby increasing its shelf life. The water vapor permeability studies, and biodegradability of the films supports its properties and proposed application. Thus SPI-CNW-AgNP nanocomposite film has immense potential for commercial application as active food packaging material. Further studies can focus on the specific interactions of the nanoparticles with the different kinds of food materials packed using the SPI film.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We have no conflicts of interest to present regarding this manuscript.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.fpsl.2022.100876.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

Supplementary material.
.

Supplementary material.
.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Shankar and Rhim, 2016
